title: The hospital that makes its own cancer drugs
text:

UT MD Anderson's Therapeutics Discovery division employs around 150 R&D scientists, who have helped progress 20 experimental drugs into clinical trials. Credit: The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer CenterEven when cancer treatments fail, there is knowledge to be gained. These experiences can generate data to reveal how tumours evolve, evade drugs and expose new vulnerabilities. At The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, researchers work to turn those signals into new therapies through the hospital’s drug discovery engine: the Therapeutics Discovery division.The division was established in 2012 to bring drug development closer to the clinical realities of an academic medical centre. Structured as a biotech-style research unit embedded within the world’s largest dedicated cancer hospital, it integrates pharmaceutical R&D with the discovery science and clinical research that UT MD Anderson is known for. The goal is to translate insights from real-world data into new therapeutic opportunities. “Therapeutics Discovery was purpose-built with end-to-end capabilities, to innovate across the continuum from discovery science through clinical impact,” says Tim Heffernan, head of oncology R&D for the division. “By engaging scientists and clinical researchers across the institution, we aim to fundamentally change the way we think about drug discovery and development.”AI at the heartThe division employs about 150 R&D scientists, many recruited from industry, with expertise spanning cancer biology, medicinal chemistry, protein engineering, data science and translational medicine. Over the past decade, they have collectively helped develop 20 experimental medicines that have advanced into clinical trials. These range from small molecules targeting DNA repair pathways to cell therapies and advanced biologics engineered to harness immune responses. The R&D engine has continued to evolve, with a preclinical portfolio that includes targeted radiopharmaceuticals that deliver radiation directly to tumours and small molecules that interfere with signalling proteins long considered difficult to drug.Yet translating biological insight into effective therapies remains difficult. Cancer’s complexity makes it challenging to predict which molecular targets will prove clinically meaningful. Modern oncology generates vast quantities of genomic, molecular and clinical data that could help reveal patterns of vulnerability — if researchers can interpret those signals.At the Therapeutics Discovery division, AI is becoming central to that effort. Advances in machine learning now make it possible to integrate diverse data sets and identify relationships that might otherwise remain hidden within the heterogeneity of cancer. For Heffernan, such tools are not simply a way to accelerate workflows, but a means of improving how therapeutic hypotheses are formed in the first place.“As technology evolves and the pace of innovation accelerates, AI must inform every decision we’re making, from target selection through drug discovery and clinical development,” he says.Some of these tools are being developed internally, trained on molecular and clinical data generated from UT MD Anderson’s large and diverse patient population. ‘Foundation’ models trained on digitized pathology images and linked genomic and clinical records are being built as general-purpose systems that can be applied across multiple cancer types, with the aim of identifying features associated with therapeutic response or resistance.An immunofluorescent image of cells, typically used to check for drug uptake and immune activation. Credit: The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer CenterInnovation through collaborationOther AI models are being developed through collaborations focused on designing new proteins and therapeutic molecules. Working with leading biotech companies, researchers in the Therapeutics Discovery division are applying machine-learning approaches to predict molecular structures, binding interactions and functional activity prior to experimental testing. According to Heffernan, the first AI-designed drug candidates from these efforts are on track to enter human trials early next year.Such collaborations are core to the division’s approach to drug discovery, notes Emily Barnhill, vice president of strategic industry ventures at UT MD Anderson. From the outset, the division was designed to be funded in part through its own outputs, with industry collaborations providing both scientific input and financial support to maintain the scale of its operations. “We’ve always envisioned this as something that could sustain itself through strategic collaborations, licensing and the advancement of assets,” Barnhill says.Those collaborations are wide-ranging. Some are early-stage discovery efforts aimed at expanding the universe of druggable targets. Others involve co-developing experimental therapies alongside biopharmaceutical companies, with shared responsibility for advancing candidates into the clinic. Still others focus on interrogating external pipelines, using UT MD Anderson’s clinical and laboratory expertise to understand how drugs work, why they fail and how they might be improved.But in all cases, the work is structured as a true collaboration rather than a service. “These are joint programmes where our scientists and clinicians are helping shape the strategy,” Barnhill says.Lab to clinic, and backTimothy Yap, head of clinical development in the Therapeutics Discovery division, is one such influential figure. A physician-scientist who specializes in early-phase clinical trials, Yap’s focus has been on targeting various DNA repair pathways that tumours depend on to survive genetic damage.The programme dates back to the founding of the Therapeutics Discovery division, before advanced AI models. Instead, it drew on a combination of biological insight, preclinical modelling and careful clinical testing. Yap had been evaluating drug inhibitors of a protein called ATR, which helps cancer cells cope with the stress of rapid growth and DNA damage. In trial after trial, across compounds developed by multiple pharmaceutical companies, he saw hints of activity, but not enough to deliver consistent clinical benefit.“Everyone was knocking on the door, but no one was getting through it,” he says.Yap and his colleagues went back to the underlying biology. Working across departments at UT MD Anderson, they analysed tumour samples and clinical data to understand why some patients responded while others did not. That effort pointed to a more precise method of selecting patients, based not just on genetic mutations but on complete loss of key DNA repair proteins, such as ATM immunohistochemistry1.“The molecular biomarker of response is really important,” says Yap.Chemists on the Therapeutics Discovery team worked to design a compound with distinct pharmacological properties, including a short-acting ‘hit-and-run’ profile intended to maximize tumour killing while limiting toxicity2. The result was alnodesertib, a drug later licensed to Artios Pharma for clinical development.Alnodesertib is now being paired with low doses of chemotherapy in clinical trials, a strategy informed by both lab and clinical insights and designed to increase replication stress on cancer cells while preserving tolerability.The effort illustrates how tightly integrated teams of the kind found at UT MD Anderson can move from biological insight to a more refined therapeutic strategy. “Drug discovery has always been a relay race,” says Heffernan, explaining that academic labs might identify targets, pass them off to pharmaceutical companies for drug discovery and development, before clinicians at a cancer centre like UT MD Anderson trial those therapies in patients. At the Therapeutics Discovery division, however, researchers hold the baton throughout, running discovery, translation and early clinical trials as one continuous race to clinical impact.
Find out more about UT MD Anderson’s Therapeutics Discovery division.For the latest on the breakthrough science transforming cancer treatment, register for the Nature Conference on Next-Generation Cancer Therapeutics, in partnership with UT MD Anderson Cancer Center.
